title: Fundamental Limitations of Post-Quantum Cryptographic Architectures
authors: Jiho Jung, Donghwa Ji, Mingyu Lee, Kabgyun Jeong
abstract: Modern lattice-based cryptography, particularly the learning with errors paradigm, relies on injecting artificial noise to secure data against quantum adversaries. This study systematically examines the theoretical and physical boundaries of this noise-reliant model across four interconnected domains: computational complexity, information-theoretic thermodynamics, quantum error correction, and quantum learning theory. Starting from the algorithmic foundation, our analysis notes that these frameworks rely on provisional complexity-theoretic assumptions that remain vulnerable to future quantum algorithmic advancements. Furthermore, by translating this cryptographic mechanism into physical thermodynamics, we illustrate that intentionally injected discrete Gaussian noise does not equate to the permanent erasure of information. Because the structural integrity of the cryptographic secret remains preserved within the ciphertext, advanced quantum error correction protocols and quantum learning models can efficiently extract the underlying mathematical kernel. Ultimately, we suggest that while lattice-based cryptography provides a robust transitional alternative, definitively classifying these frameworks as unconditionally post-quantum represents a premature classification relying on transient physical bottlenecks rather than impenetrable theoretical boundaries.
summary: This paper argues that the security of lattice-based post-quantum cryptography is not mathematically absolute but depends on the current difficulty of performing complex quantum state preparations. By framing LWE noise as a type of error that can be corrected using GKP-style protocols, the authors suggest that advancements in quantum error correction could eventually undermine these cryptographic foundations.
main_problem: The paper challenges the claim that lattice-based cryptography, specifically the Learning with Errors (LWE) paradigm, is unconditionally secure against quantum adversaries.
main_result: The authors argue that LWE security relies on transient physical and engineering bottlenecks, such as the difficulty of state preparation and QRAM access, rather than an impenetrable mathematical boundary, as the injected noise can be treated as a correctable displacement error.
method: The study uses a combination of quantum complexity theory (T-depth analysis), information-theoretic thermodynamics (Landauer's principle), and quantum error correction analogies (GKP codes) to analyze the vulnerability of LWE.
model_or_system: The model treats LWE samples as a coherent physical superposition state and maps the cryptographic Gaussian noise to continuous-variable displacement errors in a quantum Hilbert space, comparable to GKP error-correcting architectures.
key_observables: Probability amplitude of the target basis state, interference patterns of the error distribution, and T-depth complexity.
important_parameters: Integer modulus q, dimension n, error distribution chi, and the T-depth of quantum gate layers.
main_assumptions: The argument assumes that a sufficiently coherent quantum computer can successfully map classical LWE instances into a continuous-variable Hilbert space.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the algebraic structure of the LWE problem with added error; Figure 2 illustrates complexity classes and the LWE/BQP relationship; Figure 3 compares Shannon's communication model to the LWE framework.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the algebraic definition of LWE to a quantum superposition model, applies the QFT to demonstrate error amplification, identifies state preparation as a physical bottleneck, and concludes by mapping LWE to error-correctable continuous-variable systems.
why_interesting: It provides a deep connection between cryptographic noise and physical noise in continuous-variable quantum systems, suggesting that quantum error correction protocols could be used to break lattice-based encryption.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The algebraic structure of the LWE problem, where an artificial error vector e is added to the linear combination of matrix A and secret vector s (b = As + e) to mimic a noisy linear system, rendering the exact inversion of s from b computationally intractable under standard classical algorithms.

FIG. 2: Conceptual diagram of computational complexity classes. The LWE problem belongs to NP, but it is not NP-Complete. The entire security paradigm of current post-quantum cryptography relies precariously on the unproven assumption that specific lattice problems do not intersect with the BQP class.

FIG. 3: Structural correspondence between Shannon’s Communication Model and LWE. While the algorithmic error term (e) is designed to obstruct cryptographic inversion, it functions strictly as channel noise from an information-theoretic standpoint. Shannon’s theorem dictates that noise within the channel capacity is fundamentally correctable through optimal decoding.
